note: Track B SETI cadence deck. 'truth' is candidate (signal in ON only) / interference (in OFF too) / none (noise). Training cards are synthetic with known answers; real cards are famous historical cases.

real.name: The Wow! Signal
real.source: Real event · Big Ear Radio Observatory, Ohio State, 1977
real.why: A strong, narrowband burst right near the 1420 MHz 'water hole' frequency, in the beam for ~72 seconds and gone. It behaved exactly like a candidate should — but it never repeated, so it was never confirmed. Still the most famous unexplained signal in SETI history.
real.story: On Aug 15, 1977, astronomer Jerry Ehman circled six characters on a printout and wrote 'Wow!' in the margin. Decades of searches have never seen it again. Unconfirmed — not proof of anything — but a perfect example of the shape we hunt for.

real.name: BLC1 — toward Proxima Centauri
real.source: Real event · Breakthrough Listen, Parkes telescope, 2019 (analyzed 2020–21)
real.why: For months this looked like the real thing: a pure narrowband tone from our nearest neighbor star. But careful cadence analysis found look-alike signals in the OFF pointings — the mark of interference. It was ruled out as human radio junk. This is the process working.
real.story: 'Breakthrough Listen Candidate 1' was the most promising SETI hit in years. The team didn't announce a discovery — they did the hard vetting, found matching tones where there shouldn't be any, and published that it was interference. Ruling it out was the science.

real.name: Tabby's Star (KIC 8462852)
real.source: Real target · searched by Breakthrough Listen, Green Bank, 2016
real.why: Its weird, deep flickers set off 'alien megastructure' headlines. So SETI pointed the big dish at it and listened. Result: silence — no narrowband signals. The dips turned out to be ordinary dust. A quiet star is still a real answer.
real.story: KIC 8462852 dimmed in strange, irregular ways no planet could explain, and 'megastructure' theories went viral in 2015. Radio SETI found nothing artificial; follow-up showed uneven dust clouds. Honest ending — and exactly why we check instead of assume.

training.name: Target URS-101
training.source: Training signal · modeled on Breakthrough Listen C-band cadence
training.why: A steady, narrow, drifting tone that appears ONLY when the dish points at the star. That 'in the ON pointings only' fingerprint is exactly what a signal from that star should do.

training.name: Target COR-102
training.source: Training signal · modeled on Breakthrough Listen S-band cadence
training.why: The signal is also there when the telescope looks AWAY from the star (the OFF pointings). A real cosmic source can't do that — so this is radio interference from here on Earth.

training.name: Target COR-103
training.source: Training signal · modeled on Breakthrough Listen X-band cadence
training.why: No coherent signal — only the receiver's own noise. Ruling a star out is a real, publishable result: it's one more place the galaxy is quiet.
